div.h1: Cache Access Pattern: vEB vs Pointer BST
div.p: Side-by-side comparison of cache lines touched during search operations. Intel Optimization Reference Manual Style
vEB (Van Emde Boas) layouts eliminate the "pointer chasing" bottleneck by aligning the data structure topology with hardware cache line boundaries (typically 64B).

ul.li: vEB Advantage: L1/L2 Prefetchers successfully predict subsequent node accesses because they are spatially adjacent in memory.
Pointer Penalty: In standard BSTs, every pointer dereference effectively stalls the CPU pipeline for ~200-300 cycles as it waits for a main memory (DRAM) fetch, bypassing the L1/L2/L3 hierarchy.

div.p: 3-4 Cache Lines (CL) per Search Recursive subtrees fit contiguously:

ul.li: High spatial locality: Prefetcher (L2/L3) identifies stride.

div.p: ~20 Cache Lines (CL) per Search Nodes scattered across DRAM pages:

ul.li: Pointer Chasing: CPU Pipeline Stalls (300+ cycles)
TLB Pressure: Random virtual memory translations.

g.text: Offset | Data Segment | Cache Line Boundary

div.p: Latency Comparison (1M Nodes) vEB Search: ~120ns (Cache Optimized) BST Search: ~840ns (Pointer Chasing) Result: 7x Speedup
